content: The critical orientation shift organizations must make is moving from treating AI as an IT initiative — something rolled out to specific teams or functions — to treating it as everyone's responsibility. As long as AI transformation lives in the IT department, it will remain narrow and shallow rather than becoming a genuine cultural capability.
original_quote: You have to shift. The first kind of orientation shift is this is an IT initiative, right? It's like rolling out, you know, whatever — to this is about everyone.
implication: Reframing AI from an IT project to a company-wide capability shift changes who owns the initiative, how success is measured, and how quickly adoption can spread. Leaders who keep AI in IT are capping their organization's transformation potential.

content: Learning with AI happens through doing, not through training programs or observation. You cannot develop organizational AI capability by studying AI from a distance — capability compounds only through daily, hands-on engagement. The principle 'you learn as you do' applies with particular force to AI, because the technology's value is only visible through personal use.
original_quote: You learn as you do. How can we be an AI culture if we aren't all using AI every single day?
implication: Organizations that invest heavily in AI training before encouraging daily use are sequencing incorrectly. The fastest path to capability is putting AI in people's hands on real problems immediately, not prerequisite coursework.

content: AI is enabling companies to simulate customer research at scale by generating synthetic personas and querying them — replacing or augmenting expensive, slow traditional research methods. AI can impersonate a vast range of customer types with enough fidelity to generate directionally useful signal about product reception, dramatically compressing the research cycle.
original_quote: They're now simulating customer research using all sorts of incredible synthetic data because AI is actually really good at impersonating someone. You can do that across a million different customer personas and then ask them what they think about this new product offer.
implication: The cost and time barriers to customer research are collapsing, which means organizations that still rely solely on traditional research methods will be outpaced by those leveraging AI-generated synthetic insight — especially for early-stage concept validation.

content: The convergence of multiple senior leaders across different companies independently arriving at the same conclusion — universal daily AI usage as the core metric — signals that this is not a single anecdote but an emerging consensus among practitioners at the frontier. When leaders from Microsoft, Qualtrics, and Instacart say the same thing without coordination, the pattern deserves serious weight.
original_quote: I heard this theme again and again and again. JJ Schwang, who's the chief architect at Instacart, said literally almost verbatim the same thing.
implication: Leaders who are skeptical about prioritizing AI adoption breadth over AI sophistication are swimming against a current of cross-industry practitioner consensus. The evidence is not theoretical — it is converging testimony from people running the transformation in real organizations.

content: Established companies with long histories and strong brand identities — like Qualtrics being known as 'the survey company' — face a particular transformation challenge: their public identity lags behind their actual strategic investments. Years of investment in AI and machine learning can be invisible to the market if the product suite hasn't caught up, creating a gap between internal capability and external perception.
original_quote: Everybody when they think of Qualtrics, they think of survey, aren't that? Isn't that you mean the survey company, right? And he said, in the last 5 to 7 years, most of our investments have actually been in machine learning and natural language processing and AI, but our product suite really isn't reflecting it.
implication: For legacy companies undergoing AI transformation, the strategic challenge is not just building new capability but making that capability visible — both to the market and internally, so that employees understand the new identity they are being asked to help build.

content: AI-powered conversational interfaces are demonstrating measurable superiority over traditional survey-based feedback methods in customer experience contexts. When AI resolves a customer complaint within the conversation itself, it cuts the resulting NPS drop in half — and addressing it further within the same conversation cuts it in half again. This quantified impact illustrates why companies are moving from passive data collection to active AI-mediated engagement.
original_quote: If there's an NPS drop due to a dissatisfied customer, the ability of AI to resolve their complaint in the conversation reduces the drop in half. And if they then address it, it reduces that drop again in half within the conversation.
implication: Organizations still relying on post-interaction surveys to capture and address customer dissatisfaction are structurally slower than those using AI to detect and resolve issues in real time. The competitive advantage of in-conversation AI resolution is measurable and compounding.

content: Jeremy's work exists at the intersection of organizational application and student education — each context continuously informs and improves the other. Teaching student leaders and working with corporations creates a reinforcing feedback loop that deepens his understanding of design thinking in both domains.
original_quote: I find that my work with organizations really informs how I approach students and my work with students really informs how I then approach organizational challenges.
implication: Practitioners who operate across both academic and applied settings develop richer, more tested frameworks than those confined to only one context.

content: Design thinking's primary goal is to understand an end user — whether an external customer or internal constituent — better than they understand themselves. This depth of empathy enables teams to address latent, implicit needs the user may not even be aware of.
original_quote: Design thinking is about understanding an end user of a product or service — could be an external customer, could be an internal constituent — understanding them better than they understand themselves so that you might be able to address needs that are latent, that are implicit, that they may not even know about or be aware of.
implication: Surface-level user research is insufficient; the discipline demands going deeper than stated needs to uncover what people cannot yet articulate.

content: The ultimate aim of deep user understanding is to produce innovations that are truly disruptive and breakthrough — ones that delight users and exceed their expectations rather than merely satisfying stated requirements.
original_quote: With an innovation that is truly disruptive, truly breakthrough, that delights them, that exceeds your expectations.
implication: Teams that stay at the surface of user needs will produce incremental improvements; only deep empathy unlocks the possibility of genuinely surprising and delighting people.

content: Design thinking removes hierarchy and ego from the creative process by centering insights rather than ideas. When the team's shared goal is delivering breakthrough insights, individual status becomes irrelevant.
original_quote: Design thinking does a really great job of removing hierarchy from a team and of removing ego from an idea because at the end of the day, insights matter.
implication: Leaders who want more honest creative contribution should restructure team processes around insight generation rather than idea pitching, which naturally suppresses ego-driven dynamics.

content: Ideas are cheap; insights are expensive. The real competitive advantage of design thinking is building a team's consistent capacity to generate breakthrough insights — not just generating ideas.
original_quote: Ideas are cheap, insights are expensive, and design thinking is all about getting a team to be consistently able to deliver breakthrough insights.
implication: Organizations that conflate idea generation with insight generation are investing in the wrong output; the scarce and valuable resource is the deep understanding that makes ideas meaningful.

content: The ability to routinely develop and deliver breakthrough insights is not replicable by competitors, making it a durable source of organizational advantage. Individual ideas can be copied, but the systematic capability to generate insights cannot.
original_quote: If you can deliver breakthrough insights, you'll get to ideas that may be replicable, but the ability to develop and deliver insights routinely is something that isn't replicable.
implication: Building the organizational muscle for insight generation — not protecting specific ideas — is the real strategic moat for innovative companies.

content: Expertise is increasingly becoming a liability because past knowledge is not predictive of future success. In rapidly changing environments, deep experience in what worked before can actively constrain one's ability to see new solutions.
original_quote: I don't think expertise is increasingly becoming a liability because a knowledge of what worked in the past is not predictive of what's going to work in the future and so you can't just lean on your past experience.
implication: Organizations and individuals who prize domain expertise above beginner's mindset risk being outmaneuvered by less experienced but more adaptable thinkers.

content: Effective innovation requires bringing your full self to every problem in a fresh and profound way — rather than defaulting to past templates. This demands a posture of genuine openness that expertise can erode over time.
original_quote: You actually have to believe that you can bring your full self to bear in a profound new and fresh way every time you approach a problem.
implication: Creative effectiveness is as much a belief system as a skill set — practitioners must actively maintain the conviction that fresh approaches are possible, even when experience tempts them toward familiar solutions.

content: Jeremy's primary focus as a Stanford educator has shifted toward equipping student leaders not just to use design thinking techniques themselves, but to impart those principles to others and lead teams in a design thinking manner.
original_quote: I'm primarily focused on equipping student leaders not just to engage the techniques of design thinking, but really to enable them and equip them to impart those principles to other people and to lead teams in a design thinking manner.
implication: The highest-leverage design thinking education builds multipliers — people who can spread the capability through organizations — rather than individual practitioners.

content: Design thinking is no less rigorous than finance, consulting, or other traditionally analytical disciplines. The perception of it as soft or unstructured is a misconception that dissolves on close examination.
original_quote: What I found is that design thinking is no less rigorous than anything else I'd ever done.
implication: Resistance from analytically trained professionals toward design thinking is often based on a false premise; framing it as a rigorous discipline can reduce adoption friction among skeptical audiences.

content: Effective design thinking sessions require participants to arrive with pre-formed questions rather than vague problems. Framing challenges as 'How can I get a specific person or group to engage in a specific action or change?' focuses creative energy on human behavior and concrete outcomes from the start.
original_quote: your pre-work is bring three to five simple questions to the session tomorrow... the specific format we'd like you to use for the question is how can i get a specific group or person to engage in a specific physical action or mental change
implication: Loading participants with behaviorally-framed questions before a design thinking session dramatically increases the relevance and actionability of ideation work done in the session.

content: The 'How Might We' or 'How Can I' question format is a precision instrument that narrows the solution space to human-centered, actionable territory. By specifying both the target person and the desired action or change, the question eliminates vague problem statements and forces clarity about what success looks like.
original_quote: how can i get a specific group or person to engage in a specific physical action or mental change — how can i get somebody to do something
implication: Teams and individuals who invest in crafting well-formed questions before ideating will generate solutions that are more targeted and implementable than those who brainstorm from poorly defined problems.

content: Generating good design thinking questions begins with surveying the diverse areas of your life — work projects, relationships, meetings, customers, passions — rather than fixating on a single domain. Breadth of reflection increases the probability of surfacing a genuinely important challenge to work on.
original_quote: first thing is reflect on the various areas of your life or aspects of your life... think of the various areas of your life first
implication: A broad personal inventory before question generation prevents participants from defaulting to obvious or surface-level problems and surfaces richer, more motivating design challenges.

content: After identifying an area of life or work, the productive next step is to identify a specific struggle or uncertainty within that area. The struggle or uncertainty is the raw material that, when translated into a 'how can I get someone to do something' format, becomes a tractable design challenge.
original_quote: after you've thought of those areas of your life think of a specific struggle or uncertainty related to that area... after you've picked an area of your life pick a struggle and uncertainty
implication: The two-step move — area then struggle — gives practitioners a repeatable method for converting vague dissatisfaction into concrete, workable design questions.

content: The three-step question generation process — (1) identify an area of life, (2) surface a struggle or uncertainty in that area, (3) reframe it into a behavioral 'how can I get someone to do something' question — is a teachable, repeatable method for producing design-ready challenges. This transforms amorphous frustration into structured creative prompts.
original_quote: three simple steps: first thing is reflect on the various areas of your life... then think of a specific struggle or uncertainty related to that area... then bring that struggle into the format that we're specifying
implication: Giving people a structured scaffold for question generation removes the blank-page paralysis that often prevents practitioners from engaging meaningfully with design thinking methodology.

content: Bringing multiple questions (three to five) rather than one to a design thinking session is a deliberate strategy to avoid over-committing to a single framing. Quantity of questions at the input stage parallels the logic of quantity of ideas at the ideation stage — more options means better selection.
original_quote: please bring three to five of these questions with you tomorrow
implication: Requiring multiple entry questions prevents the common failure mode of investing a full session in the wrong problem simply because it was the only one prepared.

content: Design thinking is most powerful when applied to a challenge that genuinely matters to the practitioner. The pre-work assignment is designed not just to prepare questions but to ensure that the session addresses real, personally relevant struggles rather than hypothetical or assigned problems.
original_quote: we're going to have a really great productive session about using design thinking on a challenge that matters to you
implication: Intrinsic motivation in the problem space dramatically increases participant engagement and the practical applicability of design thinking outputs.

content: Modeling the question-generation process with a personal, concrete example — rather than an abstract explanation — is a pedagogical choice that makes the method immediately accessible and replicable. Jeremy's 'students don't create, they only consume' example demonstrates exactly how to move from struggle to question.
original_quote: for me it's the fact that students aren't creating anything they're only consuming... so i might say how can i get students in my class to create videos for each other — so that would be one of the three to five questions i bring with you tomorrow
implication: Facilitators and teachers accelerate adoption of new frameworks by demonstrating the process with real personal examples rather than generic or hypothetical scenarios.

content: To generate multiple questions, practitioners should cycle through multiple life or work areas rather than mining a single domain exhaustively. Each new area visited can yield its own struggle, which in turn yields another question — creating a generative loop rather than a single linear path.
original_quote: in order to generate the next question i might visit another area of my work or life and then ask myself what's a struggle or uncertainty in that area
implication: Teaching practitioners to iterate across domains for question generation ensures they arrive at sessions with genuinely diverse challenges rather than variations on a single theme.

content: Design thinking is fundamentally a methodology for generating ideas and evaluating them — not a specialized discipline reserved for trained designers. Its goal is to make the tools and mindsets that help designers solve problems accessible to anyone in any role or organization.
original_quote: Very simply, design thinking is a way of coming up with ideas and seeing whether they're any good. Very simply, and it's meant to be not some specialized thing that's reserved for the chosen few, but a set of tools that can help anyone in any career in any organization in any role.
implication: Framing design thinking as universally accessible removes the psychological barrier that keeps non-designers from adopting its tools and mindsets.

content: The single most important variable affecting the quality of ideas is the quantity of ideas generated. Most people focus on producing better ideas when they should be focused on producing more ideas — volume is the path to quality.
original_quote: What the research indicates is that the single most important variable that affects how good your ideas are is how many ideas you have. And so while many people are really focused on coming up with better ideas, the truth is they should be coming up with more ideas. The way to get to better is actually to generate more.
implication: Teams and individuals who measure and increase idea volume will systematically outperform those who focus on idea quality from the outset.

content: The popular five-stage hexagon visualization of design thinking is misleading because it implies a linear, sequential process with a clear beginning and end. Design thinking is better understood as a set of mindsets and fluid postures rather than a fixed recipe.
original_quote: A lot of people have taken design thinking as a recipe of sorts or a formula of sorts. And they assume that if you empty the powder into the bowl and then you add water and then you beat it and add an egg, that all of a sudden a cake is going to come out. It's more a set of mindsets.
implication: Teaching design thinking as a rigid procedure produces practitioners who follow steps but lack the adaptive fluency to navigate real, messy problems.

content: Empathic observation — watching real users in context and attending to their emotions — surfaces insights that data collection and surveys cannot. Bill Pacheco noticed fear on gym users' faces, an emotional truth invisible to standard market research.
original_quote: He didn't measure how long are people on the treadmill, how much do they talk about it, it wasn't data collection. He was really looking at faces. And what he noticed was the emotion that was fear.
implication: Investing time in direct, in-context observation of users' emotional states reveals the real problem worth solving, often before any data confirms it.

content: Conviction built through direct user empathy gives innovators the confidence to persist past organizational resistance. Bill Pacheco continued developing his treadmill innovation despite explicit rejection from his boss because his firsthand knowledge of users' fear was stronger than his boss's opinion.
original_quote: What happened for Bill is he got conviction that people were afraid. People felt unsafe. And as he's watching and interacting with people in gyms, that conviction grew. There's a need for a perception of safety here that I feel is a real human need, a real human pain that deserves to be met.
implication: Deep user empathy is not just a research tool — it is a source of personal conviction that enables innovators to withstand organizational pressure to abandon promising ideas.

content: Prototyping is a tool for generating new data, not just presenting ideas. By placing prototype treadmill units in a hotel gym and watching which machine users chose, Bill Pacheco created observable behavioral evidence that overrode his boss's opinion.
original_quote: Even though Ray's got 65 patents in the space, and even though he thinks it's a bad idea, he can't deny the fact that when 30 people walked into the gym, 28 of them chose this machine. Why did they choose it? That really validated the sense of fear that Bill had picked up on.
implication: Fast, cheap prototypes that generate behavioral data are more persuasive than any argument or presentation — they shift the conversation from opinion to evidence.

content: Design thinking makes two meaningful advances over conventional problem solving: first, it advocates diverging before converging on solutions; second, it recognizes that problem finding is as important as problem solving, and that both deserve a diverge-then-converge discipline.
original_quote: Design thinking makes two meaningful advances. The first advance that we advocate is we say, hang on, before we converge on one solution, let's diverge... The other significant advance that we have made in the d.school is that we said, actually let's recognize that there's two pieces to any problem solving endeavor... problem finding.
implication: Organizations that skip problem finding and jump straight to solution convergence are systematically solving the wrong problems, no matter how good their solution-generation tools are.

content: The question is every bit as important as the answer. As John Dewey said, 'A problem well put is half solved.' Much of the real work of innovation is framing a problem worth solving before ever generating solutions.
original_quote: John Dewey, the famous American educational reformer said, 'A problem well put is half solved.' And so a lot of the efforts that we spend in the design practice... a lot of your time is actually spent framing a problem worth solving before you ever even undertake the tools to think about how you'd solve it.
implication: Teams that invest in sharpening their problem statement before generating solutions will produce more targeted and breakthrough innovations than those who rush to ideation.

content: Design thinking fluency means being able to self-navigate — knowing at any moment what you are working on (problem or solution) and how you are working (generating options or making decisions) — rather than following a prescribed sequence of steps.
original_quote: The goal is for any team to be able to be fluent about what kind of activity do I need to undertake right now to continue to advance my learning about the problem we're trying to solve or the way that we're trying to solve it.
implication: The highest form of design thinking competence is metacognitive — practitioners who can diagnose what mode of thinking a situation requires and shift accordingly will outperform those who mechanically follow a process.

content: Businesses tend to focus on outputs while designers are mindful of inputs. Seeking diverse, rich inputs — observation, conversations, field research — is what enables designers to frame interesting new questions and find non-obvious problems.
original_quote: Businesses seem to keep us focused on the product, output. It seems like you are saying the creative process needs more inputs... designers are seeking inputs to frame up interesting new questions.
implication: Organizations that only measure and manage outputs starve their innovation pipeline at the source; building deliberate input-gathering practices is a prerequisite for generating valuable new questions.

content: The average corporate brainstorm generates only two ideas. Design thinking is valuable precisely because it helps teams overcome the tendency to converge prematurely and instead generate a high volume of ideas.
original_quote: There's some research that indicates that the average corporate brainstorm generates two ideas, just two. And so what design thinking does is it helps teams overcome that tendency to settle very, very quickly.
implication: If the baseline is two ideas per brainstorm, any structured effort to increase idea volume represents a massive competitive advantage — the bar is far lower than most teams assume.

content: When a promising idea session ends without assigned ownership, a defined next step, and a clear outcome to measure, the default is inaction. Innovation operationalization requires someone to explicitly own the next step and be freed from other obligations to pursue it.
original_quote: If there's no ownership, and there's no sense of when the follow up is, then there's not going to be a new conversation unless there's new information... if you allow it to be nobody's job leaving a meeting, you shouldn't be surprised that nobody does anything.
implication: The last mile of innovation — converting promising ideas into real data — requires the same project management discipline as any other organizational priority: explicit ownership, deadlines, and capacity.

content: To create space for innovation ownership, leaders must also specify what the person will stop doing. Assigning new exploratory work without removing existing obligations is a reliable way to ensure the new work never happens.
original_quote: The other thing they'd say is what are they going to stop doing? You can't give somebody something new without also kind of drawing back some level of responsibility. So which two or three standing meetings do they not need to attend in the next week so that they can create that data?
implication: Innovation capacity is a resource allocation problem as much as a creative problem — leaders who add without subtracting are signaling that innovation is optional.

content: Using multiple selection criteria — rather than a single ranking — is a more powerful way to evaluate a large pool of ideas. Different criteria (budget feasibility, customer delight, competitive differentiation) surface different promising ideas and reveal a richer picture of the solution space.
original_quote: What Jeremy and I do a lot in classes is we might take a group of ideas and select with one selection criteria... Then we might take a different color dot or a different color post-it and sort of put tabs on other ones we might select... The point is we're slicing and dicing ideas with multiple selection criteria.
implication: Teams that reduce idea selection to a single dimension — usually feasibility or cost — systematically discard ideas that would have been breakthrough on other dimensions like customer delight or differentiation.

content: Rather than converging on a single solution early, designers build a portfolio of solutions — multiple prototypes based on different selection criteria — to generate comparative data and move from vagueness to clarity.
original_quote: How would I build a group of solutions, how about I select a bunch of different ideas based on different selection criteria. It leaves my options open, and allows me to have different prototypes out there generating comparative data and helping me see the path forward out of vagueness to clarity.
implication: Portfolio thinking in innovation — running multiple prototypes in parallel — generates richer learning faster than sequentially testing a single preferred solution.

content: Human-centered design is not the same as customer-led design. Asking customers what they want produces unreliable data because customers cannot accurately predict their own future behavior and are socially motivated to be agreeable.
original_quote: Human centered design is not customer led design. It's not taking instructions from the customer, far from it. And you have to be careful to craft experiments, really, is what we would say. It's an experimental mindset that give you a highly credible data.
implication: The discipline of design research is not about asking better questions in surveys — it is about designing interactions and experiments that generate behavioral data rather than stated preferences.

content: Survey data — asking people if they would use something — is not highly credible data because customers will say yes to avoid disappointing the person asking, and because they cannot accurately imagine a future experience. Not all data is created equal.
original_quote: You ask people what they like-- I mean, I've been the puppy dog intern before myself saying, mister, would you sign up for a magazine. Put me down for a nickel, buddy. And people say yes, but it's not really an indication of what they'll do... Customers will lie to you. Not because they're malicious, but because one, they can't imagine the truth and two, they didn't want to tell the poor puppy dog intern with a clipboard no.
implication: Organizations that make major investment decisions based on survey data about hypothetical future behavior are building on a foundation of socially desirable but predictively worthless responses.

content: A low-fidelity prototype — a curtained space, a pitcher of beer, and a cactus — can test whether a value proposition drives behavioral change (foot traffic to a floor) far more cheaply and credibly than building out the actual offering. The goal is to measure the decision, not simulate the experience.
original_quote: You can hire an hourly worker, put them at the top of the fourth floor escalator... give the intern a pitcher of beer and a cactus so it's a beer garden technically... Really quickly they'd realize that that value proposition wasn't driving foot traffic. And the big question is when people become aware of an opportunity, do they hit the fourth floor button on the elevator?
implication: The creative discipline of minimum viable prototyping — stripping a test down to the single behavioral question that matters — saves enormous investment before commitment and generates far more credible data than surveys.

content: Design thinking applies equally to process design as to product design. Process — like a customer service flow, an expense reimbursement system, or a data adoption practice — can be empathized with, prototyped, and tested using the same design methods as physical products.
original_quote: Can you use it for a process? And absolutely, you can use it for a process... Process is becoming much more actually what we use it for. If I think about many, many of the projects going on in classes at Stanford.
implication: Organizations that limit design thinking to product teams are leaving enormous value on the table — every internal process is a design challenge with users, emotional stakes, and prototypable solutions.

content: Teams working on uncertain, ambiguous design challenges benefit enormously from shared language and a consistent understanding of process. When team members can name what mode they are in — ideating, prototyping, converging — they can coordinate and critique each other's work more effectively.
original_quote: Teaching a consistent process to teams... if there's any sort of amazing insight to what the d.school has done at Stanford is it's given language to students to have consistent process... They have a consistent understanding of an approach and consistent language around it. So they can even comment on each other's work and dive in and help each other.
implication: Shared creative vocabulary is an underrated organizational asset — teams with common language for design modes navigate ambiguity faster and with less interpersonal friction.

content: When generating hundreds of ideas leads to default back to the familiar, the solution is not to generate fewer ideas but to shorten the time before prototyping begins — keeping wild ideas alive by making them tangible rather than letting them die in list form.
original_quote: Hundreds of ideas, Jonathan, I would say shorten your brainstorming time and lead time right then and there to build some of the ideas, to make some really rough prototypes, to keep alive some of the wilder ideas. And say, we're going to suspend this sort of decision making, and we're just going to build out a couple of these and make them real.
implication: The gap between ideation and prototyping is where unconventional ideas die — compressing that gap by building rough prototypes during or immediately after the session prevents premature convergence on the familiar.

content: Design thinking helps individuals inside organizations become resident experts on the people whose lives their work is meant to improve. This expertise — built through direct empathic engagement — gives them standing to challenge organizational assumptions and received wisdom.
original_quote: One of the amazing things about design is all of a sudden, you can quickly become the resident expert in the people's lives who you're hoping to transform with your work efforts.
implication: Deep user knowledge is a source of organizational authority and creative courage — practitioners who build it gain the credibility to reframe problems and resist premature convergence on familiar solutions.

content: Prototyping creates new data sets rather than finding existing ones. Designers distinguish themselves by finding clever ways to generate novel evidence — not just searching for pre-existing research — which leads to better decisions than any available data could support.
original_quote: What designers are doing-- or what individuals who don't consider themselves creative or designers before, but who are using these tools-- what designers do is they're actually finding clever ways to create new data sets, which help them make better decisions.
implication: The competitive advantage of design is not access to better data — it is the discipline of creating new data through experimentation when existing data is absent or misleading.

content: Design thinking reframes business problems from output-focused imperatives ('increase revenue,' 'sell more') to human-centered problem statements ('our users feel fear') that open up fundamentally different and more valuable solution spaces.
original_quote: Too often we sort of walk through life accepting an existing problem statement. A lot of business problem statements might be around how do we increase revenue or how do we sell more. And the reframe that happened with Bill was he learned, hey for our products, there's this emotion fear. And I'm going to actually use that to design, and ultimately design a far better product.
implication: The most valuable innovation work often happens before any solution is generated — in the reframing of the problem from a business metric to a human experience worth improving.

content: When a boss rejects an idea, they are reacting to the idea with no information — only their past experience. Prototyping and testing creates new information that bypasses opinion and makes organizational resistance harder to sustain.
original_quote: His boss is reacting to an idea, but he has no information. He doesn't understand. There's no way to contextualize the idea other than according to his own past history. And what Bill realized and what's really magical is part of the design mindset is saying, really quickly I can actually create data.
implication: The strategic response to organizational resistance is not argumentation — it is rapid evidence generation that transforms a debate about opinions into a conversation about facts.

content: Daily practice and deliberate input-gathering are foundational creative habits. Building mindfulness about inputs — spending time in the field, observing, noticing — is a trainable discipline that fuels the design process and distinguishes consistently innovative individuals.
original_quote: One of the components of the class is about daily practice, and about techniques for you as an individual to build being mindful about inputs into your practice. But designers are seeking inputs to frame up interesting new questions.
implication: Creativity is less about talent and more about the daily habit of seeking diverse inputs — practitioners who institutionalize this habit generate better problems and better ideas than those who rely on inspiration.

content: The dominant mental model of innovation — going from zero to one, from nothing to something — is a myth that disempowers people. No one actually starts from zero; breakthroughs emerge from accumulation, not from thin air. Mythologizing creativity as a rare leap from nothing prevents ordinary people from seeing themselves as capable innovators.
original_quote: I don't believe in 0 to 1 people and I think it's actually incredibly disempowering to think of oneself as not a 0 to 1 person.
implication: Reframing innovation as a cumulative, learnable process rather than a magical leap makes it accessible to everyone, not just rare geniuses.

content: The Jerry Uelsmann photography experiment demonstrates that students assigned to produce maximum quantity outperformed students assigned to produce one perfect photo — both in volume and in quality. Quantity is not the enemy of quality; it is the engine of quality. The group chasing 100 photos produced the A-grade work despite never being told quality mattered.
original_quote: There was not a single A in this half of the class. No one who was assigned to the task of take one spectacular photo did. The second surprise was lots of these people took A caliber photos per this criteria, despite the fact that they weren't told that quality mattered at all.
implication: Teams and individuals should reorient their creative goals from producing one perfect output to producing maximum volume, trusting that quality will emerge from quantity.

content: Dean Keith Simonton's research across disciplines — arts and sciences alike — found that the single greatest determinant of the quality of your ideas is the quantity of your ideas. This finding contradicts the near-universal instinct to filter for good ideas from the start. Orienting around quantity rather than quality changes how you collaborate, engage markets, and interact with partners.
original_quote: He found this — that across disciplines, the single greatest determinant of the quality of your ideas is the quantity of your ideas.
implication: Organizations and individuals should measure and optimize for idea volume as the primary leading indicator of eventual breakthrough quality.

content: Most people conflate the concept of an idea with the concept of a good idea, making quality inseparable from generation in their minds. This conflation is a root cause of underperformance in creative work. When someone asks 'how do I come up with ideas,' they almost always mean 'how do I come up with good ideas' — and that mental filter is precisely what slows them down.
original_quote: I help people come up with ideas. Right. But for most of us, the notion of quality is inseparable from the notion of an idea. And that's part of the problem. That's actually what stands in our way.
implication: Separating idea generation from idea evaluation — even conceptually — is a foundational shift that unlocks dramatically higher creative output.

content: The pipeline from raw ideas to commercially successful outcomes requires far more input than most people assume — research from IDEO's toy lab shows roughly 2000 ideas yielding 100 prototypes, yielding 5 products, yielding 1 long-term success. This pattern recurs across domains: pharmaceuticals require ~10,000 attempts, Dyson made 5,126 vacuum prototypes, Taco Bell tested 2,000+ taco shell variations. The number you need is always more than you think.
original_quote: They had about 2000 ideas to get to about 100 prototypes and about 100 prototypes converted to about five sales in market... James Dyson made 5126 prototypes of the bagless vacuum before it worked.
implication: Innovation planning and resource allocation must account for the true funnel ratios; teams that budget for only a handful of ideas are systematically under-resourced for breakthrough.

content: Ideas fall on a normal distribution: some are delightful, most are ordinary, and some are dopey — in roughly equal proportions at the tails. You cannot surgically remove the dopey ideas without also eliminating the delightful ones. Accepting dopey ideas as a necessary condition of the process is what enables access to breakthrough ideas.
original_quote: You can't get rid of only one side of the distribution. You can't do it. So your choice is, which distribution do you want? Do you want delightful ideas? You got to have dopey.
implication: Creative cultures must explicitly protect and even celebrate dopey ideas, because the willingness to be dopey is the price of admission for being delightful.

content: Steve Jobs's famous 'say no to a thousand things' quote is routinely misread. The deeper insight is that he had a thousand things to say no to in the first place — a product of daily dopey-idea sharing with Jony Ive over lunch. Focus is downstream of volume; you can only ruthlessly select if you have first ruthlessly generated.
original_quote: Every day Steve and I would have lunch. And every day Steve would say, Hey, Johnny, want to hear a dopey idea... most of the time they were terrible. But every once in a while they took the air out of the room and left us both breathless and wonder.
implication: Leaders who cite Jobs as a model of focus should equally model his daily practice of generating and sharing large volumes of imperfect ideas.

content: Innovation is not about intense focus alone — it requires playful variation as well. The myth that innovation is purely about disciplined subtraction ignores the generative, playful, high-volume ideation that precedes great selection decisions. Both poles are necessary: generating many dopey ideas AND ruthlessly selecting from them.
original_quote: It's very important to realize that innovation is not just about intense focus. It's also about playful variation as well.
implication: Innovation frameworks and leadership models should balance the celebrated discipline of selection with the undervalued practice of prolific, playful generation.

content: Most people think of creativity in terms of output, but the real leverage point is inputs. The volume and diversity of inputs — people, inspiration sources, cross-disciplinary exposure — directly constrain the quality and novelty of creative output. Shifting attention upstream to input cultivation is the most effective creative strategy.
original_quote: When most people think of creativity, they think in terms of output. That's a mistake. Think in terms of input. What are the inputs that are influencing and affecting my thinking in terms of people, in terms of inspiration.
implication: Individuals and organizations seeking creative breakthroughs should audit and deliberately expand the diversity of their input diet before optimizing their idea-generation processes.

content: An idea, neurologically, is not something created from nothing — it is a connection between two things already known. The brain cannot generate new material from scratch; it can only combine existing knowledge in unexpected ways, similar to Lego pieces. This definition demystifies creativity and makes it universally accessible.
original_quote: An idea is a connection. Full stop. Specifically, it's a connection between two things I already know... The brain is incapable of creating new material from scratch. We connect things that we already know in unexpected ways similar to Legos.
implication: If ideas are connections rather than creations ex nihilo, the practical path to more ideas is accumulating more and more diverse knowledge inputs — more Lego pieces — and actively seeking unexpected combinations.

content: The story of Bette Nesmith Graham illustrates that breakthroughs arise from cross-domain connections: a secretary frustrated with typewriter smudges heard a painter say 'painters paint over mistakes' and invented Liquid Paper. The synthesis moment came from exposure to an unrelated domain that connected to her existing problem. No one starts at zero — they connect what they already know to what they newly encounter.
original_quote: The moment that the synthesis moment, the light bulb moment was when that painter said something unrelated to her work, but that connected to her work.
implication: Deliberately seeking exposure to domains, conversations, and contexts unrelated to your current problem is a reliable strategy for generating breakthrough connections.

content: Maintaining a 'bug list' — a running log of things that bother or frustrate you — is a generative creative practice that accumulates raw material for ideas. Problems that bother you are rich sources of Lego pieces: they are needs you understand intimately and are motivated to solve. Bob McKim institutionalized this practice at Stanford decades before it became common.
original_quote: He would encourage students to keep a bug list... write down the things that bother them. If it bugs you, write it down because what do we know? It's an incredibly rich source material for ideas.
implication: Keeping a systematic bug list is one of the simplest and highest-leverage daily creative habits anyone can adopt to continuously fuel their idea-generation pipeline.

content: Making connections is not passive — it requires actively looking. Carl Duncker's radiation problem research showed that giving people analogies increased solution rates from 10% to 30-40%, but explicitly instructing them to consider how the analogy bears on the problem pushed success rates above 80%. The connections available to you are invisible unless you are intentionally searching.
original_quote: When he told people consider how the analogy has bearing on the problem, all of a sudden over 80% of people can solve it. Which is to say, what if you aren't looking? You don't make the connections that are available to you.
implication: Creative practice must include the deliberate habit of asking 'how does this connect to my problem?' — not just consuming diverse inputs but actively bridging them to current challenges.

content: Bill Bowerman's waffle iron insight — pouring polyurethane into his wife's waffle iron to create the Nike waffle trainer — was not luck. It came from a man who obsessively studied athlete performance, modified students' shoes constantly, and kept detailed journals. The waffle iron spoke to him because he was always looking; a muse only appears to those whose attention is already trained on the problem.
original_quote: To whom does a waffle iron speak?... It speaks to the person whose constant really looking for a way to solve a problem.
implication: Breakthrough insights are not random gifts — they are earned by the sustained, obsessive attention that makes you receptive to connections others walk past.

content: Innovation is not a workshop, sprint, or event — it is a daily practice, more like athletic training than a scheduled activity. Just as an athlete does curls at the grocery store with a gallon of milk, an innovator treats every problem as an opportunity to flex the creative muscle. The innovation mindset means practicing everywhere, not waiting for designated creative time.
original_quote: Innovation is not an event... It's not a workshop. It's not a sprint... It's a practice... just like playing piano, what do you do? You do your scales.
implication: Organizations that rely on quarterly innovation workshops or annual hackathons are structurally underinvesting in the daily practice that produces creative capacity.

content: A seventh-grade student defined creativity as 'doing more than the first thing you think of' — a deceptively simple and operationally precise definition. This framing transforms creativity from a mysterious trait into a practicable behavior: the act of generating at least one more idea beyond your initial response. It is both the simplest definition and the most actionable.
original_quote: Creativity is doing more than the first thing you think of. I think that's actually incredibly insightful.
implication: Framing creativity as 'doing more than the first thing you think of' gives anyone an immediately actionable practice: in any situation, ask yourself 'what else?' before committing to your first answer.

content: The 'satisficing' tendency — stopping at the first acceptable solution — is a well-documented cognitive bias. There is no empirical evidence that the first solution we think of is the best, yet we routinely stop there because generating more takes effort. Recognizing this bias is the first step to countering it with a deliberate volume orientation.
original_quote: There is no empirical evidence that suggests the first solution we think of as the best and yet is called satisficing. We often settle on the first solution we find.
implication: Building a habit of explicit 'what else?' questioning after every initial solution directly counteracts the satisficing bias and opens access to better answers.

content: A daily idea quota — the practice of deliberately generating multiple solutions to a problem every day — is the operational habit that builds innovation muscle over time. The quota reframes the goal from 'find the right answer' to 'generate a volume of possibilities,' which is the mindset shift that makes breakthroughs statistically likely rather than accidentally rare.
original_quote: We call it doing a daily idea quota, where every day you say, where am I looking for the right answer?... And instead say, I'm going to try coming up with lots of answers.
implication: Implementing a daily idea quota — even for small, mundane decisions — compounds into measurable creative capacity that separates habitual innovators from occasional ones.

content: A former student's experience generating 60 ideas to reinvent the ramen noodle experience revealed that the best ideas came at numbers 13, 27, 42, and 59 — none in the first 12. Early ideas are warm-up; breakthroughs emerge from the later, harder-won ideas that come only after you've exhausted the obvious. Commitment to volume is what grants access to the non-obvious.
original_quote: Best ideas, number 13, number 27, number 42, number 59. None of my first 12 ideas were the best.
implication: Creative practitioners should treat their first cluster of ideas as necessary warm-up rather than finished output, and push significantly beyond their initial comfort zone before evaluating.

content: Jason Maden became a legendary Nike Jordan designer by making 1,000 sketches a day for years — inspired by Michael Jordan's 1,000 free throws a day. The question is not whether prolific practice produces mastery, but which comes first: was he prolific because he was talented, or talented because he was prolific? The evidence points consistently to the latter.
original_quote: Did he make a thousand sketches a day because he's Jason Madden or is he Jason Madden because he made a thousand sketches a day? The thing you practice is what enables you to become.
implication: Creative identity and mastery are outputs of sustained practice volume, not prerequisites for it — which means anyone can begin the practice without waiting to feel 'creative enough.'

content: Every problem is an idea problem — problems do not yield to a single solution but to a volume of possibilities. Recognizing this shifts the orientation from 'find the answer' to 'generate possibilities,' which is the mindset that produces breakthrough outcomes. Even mundane problems like grocery carrying are idea problems when approached with this lens.
original_quote: The good news is every problem we face is an idea problem meaning when you realize that problems yield to solutions plural... Then what you do is you get in the habit of generating solutions.
implication: Treating every problem — professional and personal — as an idea problem that demands multiple solutions is the daily practice that builds durable creative capacity.

content: Arthur Koestler defined creativity as the collision of apparently unrelated frames of reference. The practical challenge, then, is how to facilitate such collisions — and the answer often involves deliberately stepping away from the immediate problem rather than intensifying focus on it. Incubation, not efficiency, is where creative breakthroughs are made.
original_quote: Arthur Kessler, the Hungarian philosopher, said creativity is the collision of apparently unrelated frames of reference. And the question is how do you facilitate such collisions?
implication: Designing for creative collision — through walks, cross-domain exposure, and deliberate incubation time — is as legitimate and important a work activity as focused problem-solving.

content: Amos Tversky's insight that 'you waste years by not being able to waste hours' captures the paradox of creative productivity: in a hyper-efficiency culture, the incubation time that appears unproductive is actually where creative synthesis happens. Being slightly underemployed — giving the mind room to wander — is a precondition for breakthrough thinking.
original_quote: The secret to doing good research is to always be a little underemployed. You waste years by not being able to waste hours.
implication: Leaders and individuals should protect 'wasted' time — walks, naps, unscheduled space — as a legitimate and essential investment in creative output rather than a productivity failure.

content: The four-stage creative process — preparation, incubation, illumination, validation — is well-established, but incubation is the most neglected stage in modern work culture. Because we are always on and hyper-efficiency oriented, we deprive our minds of the marination time that allows unexpected connections to form. Working differently — not harder — is the solution to creative blocks.
original_quote: Incubation is where the money gets made, so to speak. It's the most overlooked step today because we don't have any time to marinate... imagination demands space to sometimes make synthesis of these new connections.
implication: Organizations should explicitly design incubation time into creative work — protecting periods of apparent inactivity as essential stages in the innovation process.

content: Donald MacKinnon's study of world-renowned architects found that the most eminent ones were the most inefficient — they delayed decisions, procrastinated, and incubated. Frank Lloyd Wright famously took two naps a day while 'not working' on Fallingwater, only approaching the drafting table when forced. Breakthrough thinkers know that sometimes you have to work differently, not just harder.
original_quote: He studied world renowned architects, and he found that the most eminent architects delayed decisions... Frank Lloyd Wright famously took two naps a day while not working on Fallingwater.
implication: Evaluating creative workers purely on visible, continuous effort systematically penalizes the incubation behaviors most associated with breakthrough output.

content: Innovation is about effectiveness, not efficiency. The tools, behaviors, and mindsets that make people creatively effective — napping, walking, playing music, juggling — are often the opposite of efficiency-oriented behaviors. Conflating productivity with efficiency is a category error that actively undermines creative capacity.
original_quote: Innovation is not about efficiency. It's about effectiveness. And the tools that are available to us to be more effective are very different than the tools and mindsets around hyper efficiency.
implication: Creative leaders need to build a new organizational vocabulary that legitimizes effectiveness-oriented behaviors — even when they look unproductive — as essential creative work.

content: The second myth of innovation — that it requires 1,000% focus and relentless elimination — is incomplete without its prerequisite: first generating thousands of options to select from. Steve Jobs's essentialism was only possible because he first generated enormous volume. The essentialism quote puts emphasis on the wrong place if taken without the generative context.
original_quote: I realized I'd be putting the emphasis on the wrong place. He said, I'm always emphasizing Steve Jobs says no, but talking with you about the ideas ratio, he said, I realize that's not the first thing. The first thing is he had a thousand things to say no to.
implication: Leaders and strategists who adopt 'focus' as their primary innovation principle without first investing in prolific generation are practicing the second half of the innovation process while skipping the first.
